coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots based on chitosan and melamine and their application in detecting vanadate (V) anions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: ALLAMBERGENOVA, Farida
SMANOVA, Zulayho
QUTLIMUROTOVA, Nigora
ABDREYMOV, Abdurakhman
SOBIROVA, Fotima
QUTLIMUROTOVA, Ruhiya
YAKHSHINOROV, Khusan
coredata.dc:description: Vanadium (V) is considered a microelement of great industrial, environmental, biological, and pharmacological importance. Vanadium-containing complexes have been widely used as biologically active compounds. Outside cells, vanadium exists in the form of the vanadate anion, while inside cells, it primarily exists as the tetravalent vanadyl cation. Both a deficiency and an excess of vanadium in the human body can lead to various diseases. Therefore, the aim of this study is to detect vanadium ions using fluorescent carbon dots, which are capable of rapid and sensitive detection. In this study, carbon dots synthesized from chitosan and melamine were used as fluorescent reagents. Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized by hydrothermally treating 99.95% pure chitosan with melamine at 180 °C for 5 h, followed by freeze-drying in a lyophilizer, and purification using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). These dots were found to emit bright green fluorescence. The formation of green fluorescence is associated with the incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon materials, which were then used for the detection of vanadium ions. The C-dots and their complexation with vanadium were fully characterized by infrared spectroscopy and spectrofluorometry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vanadium (V) ion
Luminescence
Carbon dot
Absorption spectrum
Emission spectrum
IR spectrum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Infrared spectrum of the C-dot; (b) Size distribution of the C-dot.
(a) Elemental mapping analysis of the C-dots. (b–d) Elemental mapping images of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen.
Excitation and emission spectra of the C-dot.
Excitation and emission spectra of the V5+ ion when added to the C-dot in an alkaline medium.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots when various concentrations of V5+ ions are added to aqueous solutions. (b) Dependence of concentration on analytical signal.
Effect of interfering ions on the determination of vanadium ion by C-dots.
Determining the accuracy of the method for detecting vanadate anions with C-dots.
Detection of vanadate anion in wastewater composition (n = 3, P = 0.95).

row.entry: Amount of vanadium found Xi, µg

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots based on chitosan and melamine and their application in detecting vanadate (V) anions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vanadium (V) is considered a microelement of great industrial, environmental, biological, and pharmacological importance. Vanadium-containing complexes have been widely used as biologically active compounds. Outside cells, vanadium exists in the form of the vanadate anion, while inside cells, it primarily exists as the tetravalent vanadyl cation. Both a deficiency and an excess of vanadium in the human body can lead to various diseases. Therefore, the aim of this study is to detect vanadium ions using fluorescent carbon dots, which are capable of rapid and sensitive detection. In this study, carbon dots synthesized from chitosan and melamine were used as fluorescent reagents. Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized by hydrothermally treating 99.95% pure chitosan with melamine at 180 °C for 5 h, followed by freeze-drying in a lyophilizer, and purification using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). These dots were found to emit bright green fluorescence. The formation of green fluorescence is associated with the incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon materials, which were then used for the detection of vanadium ions. The C-dots and their complexation with vanadium were fully characterized by infrared spectroscopy and spectrofluorometry.

ce:section.ce:para:
Vanadium (V) is widely used in nuclear programs, the production of corrosion-resistant and high-speed steels, and as carbide stabilizers in steel production. About 80% of the world's vanadium production is used for metal alloys, primarily in the manufacture of stainless steel products. Vanadium and its compounds are toxic, with a toxic dose for humans being 0.25 mg. A dose of 2–4 mg of vanadium is considered extremely dangerous to life. Consequently, there has been a growing interest in research focused on detecting trace amounts of vanadium and its compounds. Vanadium and its compounds have been used pharmaceutically to reduce cholesterol and glucose levels, as antidiabetic and anticancer agents, and to treat vascular constriction [1]. Vanadium is a well-known metal with high physiological, environmental, and industrial significance [2]. It is an important microelement for human metabolism, performing biological functions in the fermentative activity of many phosphates, and playing a role in body mass formation and normal bodily functions [3]. Vanadium exists in multiple oxidation states ranging from −1 to +5, but in natural waters, it is predominantly found as VO2+ and
VO 2 +
[4]. Vanadium-based compounds have been proposed as a new class of non-platinum anticancer drugs [5]. A rapid, sensitive, and selective method for preconcentration and detection of vanadium has been described using 3-hydroxybenzaldehyde-4-aminoantipyrine (HBAP) chemically immobilized on chitosan, which has been successfully applied to water and soil samples [6]. The Coprinellus truncorum mushroom, widely distributed in Europe and North America, has absorbed a wide spectrum of heavy metals such as vanadium and has dissolved. The antidiabetic activity of vanadium-enriched mushrooms has been studied, showing a significant increase in glucose-1-phosphate (G1P) and fructose-6-phosphate (F6P) levels [7]. Strong luminescent C points have been successfully synthesized from lemon juices, which are inexpensive and readily available as starting materials. These carbon dots (C-dots) exhibited luminescence dependent on excitation wavelength, with a maximum emission at 540 nm and achieved a high quantum yield of 21% [8]. Various methods for the determination of vanadium ions are described in spectrophotometry [9–12], spectrofluorometry [13], and electrothermal atomic absorption spectroscopy (ETAAS) [14].

C-dots are a novel group of optically active nanomaterials composed of aromatic, aliphatic cores, and surface functional groups, with typical sizes < 10 nm [15], although larger C-dots up to 60 nm have been reported [16,17]. Various functional groups composed of sp² and sp³ hybridized carbon atoms are attached to the surface of amorphous or graphite-based carbon materials. Depending on the type of carbon hybridization, the degree of carbonization, the degree of crystallization, the type of interconnection, and the available functional groups, there are general terms such as carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Fig. 1 ) [18]. Due to their high fluorescence properties, ease of preparation, and affordability, C-dots hold great potential for fluorescence-based analytical applications [19,20]. C-dots can incorporate heteroatoms like N, S, P, and B. They can also be chemically modified to enhance or display additional functional properties [21]. C-dots are widely studied carbon-based materials because of their strong fluorescence with tunable emission based on their size, surface structure, and excitation wavelength [22]. Compared to organic dyes, C-dots offer superior water solubility, functionality, resistance to photobleaching, toxicity, biocompatibility, and broader photoluminescence [23]. Recent studies have shown that luminescent C-dots with different structures (Fig. 1) have been used for nanomolar detection of Fe3+ [24–28], Fe2+ [29], Ag+ [30], Cu2+ [31–33], Hg2+ [34], Zn2+ [35], Cd2+ [36], Co2+ [37], Al3+ [38] va Be2+ [39] metal ions.
In the research, C-dots synthesized based on chitosan-melamine were used to detect vanadium ions using a fluorescent method, utilizing information from the literature.
The aim of the study is to develop a method for detecting trace amounts of vanadium ions using green fluorescent carbon dots.

A Spectra Max Fluorimeter was used to record absorption and emission spectra. A scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to determine the elemental composition. The concentration and size distribution of C-dots were determined using a NanoSight NS300, while infrared spectra from 400 to 4000 cm−1 were obtained using a Bruker Invenio S FT-IR spectrometer.

All reagents and chemicals were purchased at an analytical reagent grade or higher and used without further purification. Chitosan (99%) was purchased from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China) for the synthesis of C-dots. Melamine (99%), ammonium metavanadate (99.95%), sulfuric acid, acetic acid, and other reagents were purchased from commercial sources. Deionized water was used for all analyses.

The C-dots were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. To do this, 1 g of chitosan and 1 g of melamine were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water in the presence of 2 mL of diluted acetic acid. The solution was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. The solution was then cooled to room temperature. The resulting substance was filtered to remove larger particles and centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 15 min. It was dialyzed for two days using a dialysis membrane (M W: 3500) against deionized water, and the final powdery product of the carbon dots was dried using a lyophilizer (BIOBASE BK-FD50S Pilot Freeze Dryer) for subsequent analysis. The obtained C-dots exhibited blue emission under 412 nm UV light. The synthesis process sequence is shown in Fig. 2 .
To study the properties of the C-dots, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), fluorescence spectra, NanoSight (NTA), and SEM were recorded.

Infrared measurements were used to study the nature of the functional groups present in the C-dots (Fig. 3 a). According to the literature, the OH group in C-dots appears at a vibration frequency of 3310 cm−1 [40], and the NH group appears at 3224 cm−1 [41]. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of C-dots in an aqueous solution. The peak at 2830 cm⁻¹ on the surface of the C-dots corresponds to the vibrational frequency of the CH group in the aromatic ring, while the peaks at 2484.08 and 2097.25 cm⁻¹ correspond to the C=N group in the aromatic ring. The peak at 1634 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the carbonyl group C=O in the COOH group [42], the peak at 1505 cm⁻¹ corresponds to C=C [43], the peak at 1458 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the CC group [44], the peaks at 1431 and 1403 cm⁻¹ correspond to the CN group, the peaks at 1328 cm⁻¹ and 1245 cm⁻¹ correspond to the arylamine CN group, the peak at 1066 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the CO group [45], and the peak at 978 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the CH [46] vibrational frequency. FT-IR spectroscopy analysis showed the presence of numerous hydroxyl, amino acid, and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the C-dots.

NTA was performed at 298 K to determine the concentration and size distribution of the C-dots. The synthesized C-dots were primarily distributed in the size range of 1–100 nm, with the majority of the concentration observed in the 1–30 nm range (Fig. 3b), indicating that many particles were widely distributed within this size range.

The SEM image of the synthesized C-dots was obtained. According to the SEM results, it can be seen from Fig. 4 a–c, d that the C-dot consists of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, and has a spherical shape.

The most important characteristic of the synthesized C-dots is their fluorescent property. Therefore, 0.001 g of the carbon dots were weighed on an analytical balance and dissolved in bidistilled water in a 100 mL flask to prepare a 0.001% solution. The fluorescence intensity of this solution was studied using a Spectra Max Fluorimeter (Fig. 5 ).

From Fig. 5, it can be seen that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the C-dot are 338 and 412 nm, respectively, indicating its high fluorescence and suitability for detecting metal ions.

The wavelength difference between the absorption and emission spectra is Naeemullah and Tuzen [46]:
(1)
B

λ k

Using the formula above, the quantum yield of the C-dot was calculated and found to be 0.82. This proves that the C-dot has a high light emission intensity.
(2)
φ =
E l y u m .
E
a b s .
= 08204

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of vanadium ions in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The environment influences the formation of complexes. Therefore, the effect of the environment on the formation of complexes between the C-dot and vanadate anion was studied. In this experiment, 0.1 µL of Britton-Robinson buffer solution with pH values of 2.17, 5.27, and 9.87, 0.1 µL of C-dot, and 0.1 µL of vanadate anion were added to a cuvette, mixed, and their absorbsion was examined. The results show that the fluorescence of the C-dot with the vanadate anion decreases at pH = 9.87. In an alkaline environment, the C-dot fully forms a complex with the vanadate anion. The fluorescence quenching is observed due to the binding of the C-dot with the vanadate, and this is presented in Fig. 6 .
Due to the stability of the vanadate anion in alkaline conditions, it forms a complex with the C-dot, which is evidenced by the decrease in fluorescence intensity from 1650 to 450.

To determine the linearity of vanadate detection with the C-dot, the concentration of the vanadate anion was gradually increased. In this case, 0.1 µL of Britton-Robinson buffer solution with a pH of 9.87, 0.1 µL of C-dot, and 0.1 µL of vanadate anion solutions ranging from 100 to 700 ppm were added to the cuvette, mixed, and their fluorescence was examined. The results are presented in Fig. 7 a.

The figure shows that a linear correlation exists between the analytical signal and the amount of the substance when detecting vanadium ions with the C-dot, occurring at vanadium concentrations from 100 to 700 µg in a 0.1 µL solution. Higher amounts lead to a decrease in fluorescence, so the amounts within this range were used in subsequent experiments. This demonstrates that the sensitivity for detecting micromolar quantities of vanadium is sufficient.

To validate the accuracy of the developed method, a calibration graph was utilized, revealing that the correlation coefficient around 100–700 µg of vanadium in a 0.1 µL solution is 0.9994, indicating that the developed method possesses high precision (Fig. 7b).

The selectivity of the developed method was assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensities of carbon dots in the presence of vanadium and foreign ions. A solution containing 10 µg of 0.1 µL vanadate anion, 0.001% C-dots at 0.1 µL, and 10 µg of 0.1 µL foreign ions was prepared using a Britton-Robinson buffer solution at pH 9.87. The mixture was then stirred, and the fluorescence was analyzed, with the results presented in Fig. 8 .

The results show that the detection of 10 µg of vanadate anion was only hindered by the presence of chromate and manganate ions at the same concentration, while the other ions did not interfere. To reduce the interference from chromate and manganate ions, they were initially converted back to cationic form using hydrogen peroxide, resulting in their precipitation in an alkaline medium, which does not hinder the detection of vanadate anions. This highlights the high selectivity of the method.

Taking into account the selected optimal conditions, the developed method was applied to the analysis of wastewater around the Almalyk Mining and Metallurgical Combine and compared with ISP-OS analysis results.

A sample of 0.1 µL of wastewater was taken, to which 0.1 µL of C-dots was added, along with Britton-Robinson buffer solution at pH 9.87, and the fluorescence intensities were measured. Since the medium was alkaline, metals such as iron, tellurium, and lead precipitated out of the solution and did not interfere with the detection of vanadate anions. The results were repeated three times and compared with the ISP-OS results. Fisher parameters were calculated, and the obtained results are presented in the table.

Based on the table results, the calculated Student's coefficient and Fisher criterion values were found to be tphis = 0.36 and Fₕᵢₛ = 1. The values from the table are smaller, which proves the accuracy of the developed method and indicates that there is no systematic error.

In conclusion, C-dots were synthesized from chitosan and melamine through a hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 5 h, and their fluorescent properties were characterized. It was found that the maximum absorption wavelength of the C-dots was 338 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength was 412 nm. The synthesized carbon dots were dialyzed to achieve a uniform nanoparticle size of < 100 nm. The composition, shape, and nature of the functional groups of the carbon dots were examined using FT-IR, SEM methods, confirming their spherical shape and the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups. It was observed that these functional groups formed complexes with V5+ ions in an alkaline medium, leading to fluorescence quenching. Based on this property, the method was recommended for the detection of vanadate anions. The results of detecting vanadate anions using the developed method showed a linear relationship between the analytical signal and concentration in the range of 100–700 µg at 0.1 mL, with a correlation coefficient close to 1, proving the accuracy of the method. Interfering ions in the detection of vanadate anions were identified, and hydrogen peroxide solution was used to eliminate them. The developed method was applied to wastewater analysis and demonstrated high sensitivity (Tables 1 and 2
).

Farida ALLAMBERGENOVA: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Zulayho SMANOVA: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nigora QUTLIMUROTOVA: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Abdurakhman ABDREYMOV: Project administration, Investigation. Fotima SOBIROVA: Software. Ruhiya QUTLIMUROTOVA: Validation, Software. Khusan YAKHSHINOROV: Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that the research was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial relationships that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest.
